Do you think this may be even more efficient than simply redirecting simple airflow or am I right in thinking this is simply me over engineering something?

author: @glencahalin4786
text: I was thinking changing the angle and slightly overlap them as a single unit rather than 2 separate pieces each  The thought being that the inside front of the following blade would then also be directing the air into the inside gap of the blade in front, adding to the air going through it, rather than letting it escape through the current gap. Essentially the blades become ( slightly asymmetric ) curved arrowheads  pointing into the Pelton aspect of the arrowhead in front

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: Actually it might prove more efficient to not have the apex of the trailing blade exactly centre of the leading indentation ( or indeed either of them being symmetrical ) but for a first principle test it'd do and those more well versed in fluid dynamics might be able to optimise the 2 shapes and positionings. but I also thing such a design would benefit from a donut  top cover over the actual arrowheads, to stop the head on wind getting straight into the "jetting" section. Which would probably also apply to the current design.

author: @rudolfvanacker8569
text: At video 1:44 the rotor end-view appears to show that the pelton split ridge causes an obstruction to the airflow from the central area to the outside of the airfoil. A possible modification would be to change it into a plain U-shape with the inner leg slightly inclined (say 15deg or so) towards the centre to improve the pick-up of the airflow from the proposed central cone. The airfoil could also be extended towards the centre of the U to improve the airflow in that area, and increase its speed over the airfoil.

author: @ryandavis7593
text:
I am glad to see someone do what I did in 2005 and couldn’t get anyone on board. 
BTW, if you use springs on the airfoils you can make it self governing.

replies.author: @gregorpeters5042
replies.text: Hello , I'm actually trying to build this blade design at the moment and I'm interested to hear what you mean by this ? - do you mean self governing in the sense of pitch angle?

replies.author: @ryandavis7593
replies.text:
@ 
Thank you for your interest. Each blade is able to pivot on its center of lift. A set of torsion springs on each wing pivot causes them to have a greater pitch angle increasing the lift of each wing/blade. My original had a rod on each wing that would be forced outwards by the centrifugal force of the turbine over revving.
So yes. Pitch angle is reduced by increased RPM.

author: @Arturo-lapaz
text:
In engineering, the important thing is  not only to understand the object but to reliably predict the performance.
Researchers did just that, in case of the horizontal axis wind turbine, a much improved windmill, don Quixote and his minion Sancho Panza tried to lance in La Mancha.
Mr Betz, soon to become Dr Betz came up with an equation, which describes the absolute maximum a windmill  can extract from the wind.
He measured and observed and tested and calculated and observed that in this ideal case the wind about to enter into the disc of the rotor, slows down to 2/3 its velocity, when passing through that area swept by the long and narrow blades. After that,  the wake, as it is now called slows down to a third of the wind speed, but only if the maximum energy is to be gained. 
He also found that the force transmitted to the mast of the tower is 2/3 of the windspeed squared times the swept area of all blades and times the air density, which at sealevel is 1.225 kg/m³
Now this force is pushing the tower back, like a sail on a boat runnig from the wind. The power in the best case is simply the product of this force times the 2/3 of the wind so, inescapably the power upper limit is
density × (2/3 wind)³ × disc area
where the disc area is π × blade lenght².
This is so simple, so very simple that only a child can do it.
( Tom Lehrer, New math )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @Arturo-lapaz
replies.text:
​​​@ThinkingandTinkeringThanks, some humor helps with Cervantes the writer.

author: @thesustainabilitysoonerchannel
text: Robert, thanks to your videos, we have been experimenting w/various combinations of the Waters Turbine and Darwin Collector.  The moment you brilliantly started work on the morphing the Pelton wheel into airfoils, we knew you were onto something and immediately started thinking of how to combine all 3.  We're highly confident that's a viable path toward a very impressive TSR.  Way beyond 5 and possibly into the stratosphere.  It's also great to see that a mere 1 meter per second breeze gets the Pelton Turbine moving.  We're currently down a key team member.  Our main 3D designer-printer has pealed-off to his own entrepreneurial venture in the marine industry and we wish him well.  In the meantime, we can't wait to see what comes next in this video series.  Best wishes, Your Fans @ SustainabilitySooner here in the USA😊

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @mliittsc63
text: I'm wondering if changing the angle of the "straight" (actually slightly curved) blades might make it more efficient. As they are now, it looks like there would be some turbulence created at the trailing edge, though with sufficient speed, centrifugal force might take care of that naturally. Also, it would be interesting to see the effect of the Darwin Collector if it were mounted on top of a tall "chimney" with an axial flow turbine at the bottom. Would also work with a radial flow turbine with proper ducting like you get with a turbocharger turbine (more torque this way). Either way, it might achieve some of the benefits of a standard giant-size axial flow turbine without the complications of wind following, putting the generator high up, and manufacturing/maintaining/transporting huge blades. Probably kill fewer birds, and maybe make less noise.

author: @HomoInsanus
text: I might be wrong but it seams that trying to split the stream of air in two jets is a rudiment that is left of the Pelton Turbine design. I think that making it a single parabola will make the air flow more efficient but the design will look a bit less fancy. The air much easier will be redirected behind the air foil/fin, in the low pressure zone inside the turbine. In the current design a portion of air is always redirected back against the wind, which impacts the efficiency. Should be easy enough to test.

author: @fabiosogni3420
text:
I see a trend and I'd like to push it forward.
The trend I see is to combine different solutions to generate power and different solutions to improve efficiency.
So why not putting the concept to the extreme? 
Why not combining all of this? Something like a rotating-oscillating wind generator covered with solar and peltier cells and well...all sitting on a stirling engine (whatever that means). 
Of course this Frankenstein device uses magnetic bearings to reduce friction and other crazy stuff.

Ok, let me have a cold drink, it is damn hot here in southern Germany ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that would be an awesome creation!

replies.author: @fabiosogni3420
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering certainly awsome, but also useful?
If you "add up" low-efficiency systems, do you get close to 100% or do you stay under an inefficiency ceiling?
I didn't study physics enough...

Surely such a monster would compensate for the external conditions (no sun? ok, but it's windy. No sun and wind? Ok, but there's heat, etc..) but would the total efficiency increase? Would it still be worth it?

Just food for though.
